root_motivation: Concurrent Claude sessions on the same laptop are mutually blind, leading to clobbered shared files (settings.json, recall DB) and redundant work. agorabus provides an advisory presence+pub/sub substrate over a Unix-domain socket so co-located sessions can announce themselves, see peers, and exchange low-volume coordination events.
user_persona: Joe Yen running multiple concurrent Claude Code sessions on a single Linux laptop (single-user trust model). The CLI is invoked by SessionStart/Stop hooks and by Claude itself during a session for ad-hoc peer queries.

acceptance_criteria.description: `agorabus daemon` starts a UDS server at ~/.cache/agorabus/sock (or path from --socket), creates parent dirs with 0700, sets the socket file mode to 0600, and accepts at least one client connection.

Newline-delimited JSON protocol: the first message from a client must be an `announce` op carrying session_id, pid, cwd; the daemon replies with {"ok":true} and records the peer. Non-announce first messages get {"ok":false,"error":"announce_required"}.
`agorabus peers --socket <path>` returns a JSON array of currently-connected peers with their announce records (session_id, pid, cwd, intent, last_heartbeat).

Heartbeat semantics: a client may send {"op":"heartbeat","tool":"..."} at any time; the daemon updates the peer's last_heartbeat. Peers with no heartbeat for more than the configured timeout (default 60s, override via --heartbeat-timeout) are pruned from the peers list.

Pub/sub: a client subscribed via {"op":"subscribe","prefix":"shared."} receives every subsequent {"op":"publish","topic":"shared.X","data":...} whose topic begins with that prefix. Streamed as one JSON object per line on the subscriber's socket.

Fail-open client: `agorabus peers` with no daemon running exits 0 and emits `[]` on stdout (PRD risk-mitigation requirement). All client subcommands treat connection-refused / no-socket as 'no peers / no bus' rather than as a hard error.

JSON line protocol is robust to malformed input: a client sending non-JSON or a JSON object missing `op` receives an `{"ok":false,"error":...}` reply and the connection is closed; other clients are unaffected.
Concurrent connections: the daemon serves at least 8 simultaneous clients without dropping announce records or pub/sub deliveries (proptest-style fan-out check at smaller N=4 in the acceptance test).

scope: Single multi-subcommand CLI binary `agorabus` (one of: daemon, announce, peers, publish, subscribe, heartbeat).
Unix-domain socket transport with newline-delimited JSON framing.
In-memory peer registry with heartbeat-based liveness; no on-disk persistence in v1.
Pub/sub with dotted-topic prefix subscription matching.
Fail-open client behavior: missing daemon never raises an error from a Claude session.
0600 socket permissions; relies on UDS uid filtering (single-user model).
non_goals: Strong consistency or real mutual exclusion (advisory only; real locks belong to `flock`).
Persistent ring-buffer log to ndjson (Phase 1 in the PRD; deferred — daemon is memory-only in v1).
`send` targeted messages with opt-in receive (PRD Phase 2; deferred).
Sub-agent integration / `Skill(...)` dispatch interception (PRD Phase 3; deferred).
systemd-user-socket activation (one launch mode only in v1: explicit `agorabus daemon`).
TLS, ACLs, per-topic auth, or any non-uid auth beyond UDS file permissions.
Service-target (`--target service`) under autobuilder v1: this is a multi-subcommand CLI where `daemon` is one of several subcommands of the same binary.

five_whys_trace.q: Why build a Unix-socket pub/sub bus?
five_whys_trace.a: Two concurrent Claude sessions today (PIDs 1202 and 1381) have no way to see each other and risk clobbering shared resources (settings.json, recall DB, MEMORY files).

five_whys_trace.q: Why does mutual blindness specifically matter (vs. relying on filesystem locks)?
five_whys_trace.a: fs locks address the data race but not the semantic race — two sessions can independently decide to update the same memory in incompatible ways. Need *intent* visibility, not just *write* mutex.

five_whys_trace.q: Why a long-lived bus rather than a watch-and-poll over a shared status file?
five_whys_trace.a: Heartbeat + pub/sub gives clean crash detection (no heartbeat for 60s == gone) and pushes events to subscribers in real time; polling a file misses transient publishes and leaves dead-session detection to ad-hoc TTLs.

five_whys_trace.q: Why does this need its own daemon rather than reusing dbus/systemd activation?
five_whys_trace.a: Scope is agent-traffic-shaped (announce + topic prefix subscribe + heartbeat liveness), and a ~300 LoC tokio daemon is cheaper to own than the dbus learning curve. Trust model is single-uid; UDS perms suffice.

If a Claude session ever *blocked* on the bus during its hot path, the bus would become a new SPOF for the agent. Fail-open means 'bus down == no peers known == proceed normally,' which keeps the bus advisory rather than authoritative.

ambiguities_resolved.question: PRD describes both Phase 0 (announce/peers/heartbeat) and Phases 1–4 (publish/subscribe, query-state, send, sub-agent integration, self-review). Which phases are in scope for v1?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Phase 0 + Phase 1 (publish/subscribe with prefix matching) are MUST. Phases 2–4 are explicit non-goals. The MUST-AC set reflects this scope-narrowing.

ambiguities_resolved.question: PRD calls this a 'daemon' but autobuilder v1 supports only --target cli/lib. Service target is out of scope.
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Model as a multi-subcommand CLI where `agorabus daemon` is one subcommand. Same binary serves both client and server roles. This is exactly how the PRD describes the architecture (`agorabus daemon` is mentioned as embedded in the client).

ambiguities_resolved.question: Should the ring-buffer event log (~/.cache/agorabus/log/YYYY-MM-DD.ndjson) be in v1?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: No. Deferred to a follow-up. v1 daemon is memory-only; replay-on-subscribe is not supported. This keeps the v1 surface within autobuilder's iteration budget.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Should `send` targeted messages with opt-in receive be in v1?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: No. PRD Phase 2. Pub/sub covers the coordination story; targeted send adds an inbox-subscription convention that doesn't pay off until Phase 3 sub-agent traffic exists.
